questions.q: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain?
choices: the set of closed points of $S$
isomorphism classes of families $E\to S$ whose geometric fibres are elliptic curves
the set of line bundles on $S$
the category of all schemes over $S$
questions.explain: The moduli functor sends a base $S$ to iso classes of families $E\to S$ of the objects of interest — here, flat proper families of elliptic curves.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space?
For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: $\Delta=-16(0+27)=-16\cdot 27=-432$, which is nonzero, confirming the curve is smooth (hence an elliptic curve).

hard.q: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is NOT representable. Which chain of reasoning correctly identifies the first obstruction?
choices: The $j$-invariant is not a polynomial, so no scheme can parametrise curves

There exist nontrivial isotrivial families $E\to S$ (all fibres isomorphic, but the family is non-trivial) whose classifying map $S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is constant, showing $h_{\mathbb{A}^1}\not\cong\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$
The $j$-line is non-compact, so a universal family would have to extend to $\infty$
The moduli functor sends every scheme to an empty set
hard.hint: Automorphisms create non-isomorphic families with the same $j$-invariant.
hard.explain: A representable functor $h_M$ cannot distinguish two families with the same classifying map. Twisting a constant family by a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor (at $j=0$) gives an isotrivial but non-trivial family, exposing the failure: $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\not\cong h_M$ for any scheme $M$.

hard.q: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 + x$ (so $a=1$, $b=0$). Compute the discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3+27b^2)$.

A family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E}\to S$ induces a morphism $f:S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were a fine moduli space with universal family $\mathcal{U}$, one would need $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Why does this fail for the family $y^2=x^3+t^2$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_t$?
choices: The discriminant vanishes at $t=0$
The family has constant $j$-invariant ($j=1728$) but is non-trivial as a family, so $f$ is constant while $\mathcal{E}\not\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$
The base $\mathbb{A}^1_t$ is not proper
The Weierstrass equation has wrong degree
hard.hint: Fine moduli means every family comes from a unique classifying map.
hard.explain: For $y^2=x^3+t^2$, scaling $x=u^2 x'$, $y=u^3 y'$, $t=u^3 t'$ shows constant $j=1728$, so $f:\mathbb{A}^1_t\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is constant. Yet the family is non-trivial (the twist by $t^2$ cannot be removed over $\mathbb{Z}$), contradicting $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$.

questions.q: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$?
choices: $\mathcal{M}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\bar k)\to M(\bar k)$ is a bijection for every algebraically closed $k$
$\mathcal{M}$ is naturally isomorphic to $h_M=\operatorname{Hom}(-,M)$, so there exists a universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$
$M$ is proper and smooth
$M$ is the quotient of a scheme by a finite group
questions.explain: Fine moduli is exactly representability: $\mathcal{M}\cong h_M$. The identity $\mathrm{id}_M\in h_M(M)$ corresponds to the universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$.

questions.q: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type?
choices: fine moduli
coarse moduli (no universal family)
it is not a moduli space in any sense
it is a compact moduli space
questions.explain: The $j$-line is the coarse moduli space of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: geometric points match iso classes bijectively, but no universal family exists because of automorphisms.

questions.q: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)?
questions.explain: For generic $j$, the only nontrivial automorphism is $[-1]:(x,y)\mapsto(x,-y)$, so $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|=2$.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a moduli functor with a coarse moduli space $M$. Which combination of properties does $M$ satisfy, but a fine moduli space additionally requires?
choices: Coarse: $\mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec}\bar{k})\to M(\bar{k})$ bijective for all alg. closed $k$, and $M$ is initial among such schemes. Fine additionally requires a universal family $\mathcal{U}\to M$ inducing a natural isomorphism $\mathcal{F}\cong h_M$
Coarse: $M$ is proper. Fine: $M$ is affine
Coarse: $M$ is smooth. Fine: $M$ is reduced
Both are the same; fine just has a nicer name
hard.hint: Coarse moduli universalizes on points but not on families.
hard.explain: A coarse moduli space satisfies (i) the bijection on $\bar{k}$-points and (ii) initiality. Fine moduli additionally demands representability: a universal family over $M$ inducing $\mathcal{F}\cong h_M$ as functors.

hard.q: The curve $y^2=x^3-x$ has $j=1728$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$. How many elements does $\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ have?
hard.hint: Order-$4$ automorphism comes from $(x, y) \mapsto (-x, iy)$.
hard.explain: At $j=1728$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$, strictly larger than the generic $\mathbb{Z}/2$ but smaller than $\mathbb{Z}/6$ at $j=0$.

hard.q: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is NOT a fine moduli space?
choices: Over $\mathbb{C}$, there is no map $\mathbb{A}^1_j\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$
Over $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$, the family $y^2=x^3+t$ and the constant family $y^2=x^3+1$ have the same $j$-map to $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ but are non-isomorphic as families
The $j$-invariant is not surjective
Elliptic curves over finite fields do not have $j$-invariants
hard.hint: Twist families over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k[t, t^{-1}]$ break fine representability.
hard.explain: Both families send every point to $j=0$ ($j(y^2=x^3+c)=0$ for any $c\ne 0$), yet twisting by $t$ gives a non-trivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were fine, the two families would be pulled back from the universal one via the same map and hence isomorphic — contradiction.

questions.q: Why does the existence of nontrivial automorphisms prevent $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ from being representable?
choices: Because automorphisms make the moduli functor empty

Because one can twist a trivial family by a nontrivial class in $H^1(S, \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(E))$ to get a non-isomorphic family whose fibres are still all isomorphic, invisible to any representable functor
Because automorphisms force the moduli space to be zero-dimensional
Because they make the $j$-invariant ill-defined
questions.explain: A representable functor $h_M$ can only see families that pull back from a single classifying map. Twisting by a nontrivial cocycle in $H^1(S,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}})$ produces genuinely different families with identical classifying maps, which $h_M$ cannot distinguish.

questions.q: The curve $y^2=x^3+1$ has $j=0$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of what order? (Hint: it is generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x, -y)$.)
questions.explain: At $j=0$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/6$, generated by the order-6 map $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x,-y)$ where $\zeta_3$ is a primitive cube root of unity. This is the maximum automorphism group for an elliptic curve in characteristic $\ne 2,3$.

questions.q: The family $E_2: y^2=x^3+t^6$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\setminus\{0\}$ is a nontrivial twist of $E_1: y^2=x^3+1$. The obstruction class lives in:
choices: $H^0(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\},\mathcal{O})$
$H^1(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\},\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(E_1))$
$H^2(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\},\mathbb{Z}/2)$
$\pi_0(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\})$
questions.explain: Twists of a fixed family are classified by first Čech cohomology with coefficients in the automorphism sheaf. The nontriviality of this $H^1$ is exactly the obstruction to representability.

hard.q: Order the steps in showing that $[-1]\in\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ obstructs representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$:
choices: (A) Exhibit an isotrivial family $\mathcal{E}\to S$ non-isomorphic to the constant one. (B) Show $[-1]$ acts nontrivially on the torsor over $S$. (C) Conclude $h_M\ne\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ since both families have the same classifying map.
(A) Compute $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|=2$. (B) Conclude the moduli space is 0-dimensional. (C) Use Riemann–Roch.
(A) Show $[-1]$ is the identity. (B) Conclude the functor is trivial. (C) Apply Yoneda.
(A) Compactify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$. (B) Add boundary. (C) Conclude non-representability from properness.
hard.hint: Nontrivial torsors for $[-1]$ give distinct families with the same invariant.
hard.explain: The standard three-step argument: (A) construct the twist, (B) identify $[-1]$ as the monodromy of the torsor, (C) apply Yoneda to conclude the representing object would need to distinguish the two families via the same map — impossible.

hard.q: Let $E_0: y^2=x^3+1$ (so $j=0$, $|\mathrm{Aut}|=6$) and $E_1: y^2=x^3-1$. The twist $E_1$ is a non-trivial torsor under $\mathbb{Z}/6$. Over $\mathbb{C}$, $E_0\cong E_1$ because $j(E_0)=j(E_1)=0$. How many distinct isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ have $j=0$?
hard.hint: The twist corresponds to a square root of a non-square element.
hard.explain: Over $\mathbb{C}$ the $j$-invariant is a complete isomorphism invariant, so $j=0$ corresponds to exactly one iso class. Twists become isomorphic over $\mathbb{C}$ because $\mathbb{C}^*$ is algebraically closed.

hard.q: Consider a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor $P\to S$ twisted by a class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. If $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$ and the torsor is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathcal{O}_S[u]/(u^2-t)$, this class is:
choices: trivial, since $t$ is a unit on $S$
nontrivial, since $\sqrt{t}$ does not exist globally on $S$ (it would require adjoining $\sqrt{t}$, changing the ring)
trivial for all $t\ne 0$ by the implicit function theorem
nontrivial only in characteristic $2$
hard.hint: Monodromy around the puncture detects the nontrivial torsor.
hard.explain: $\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\})=k[t,t^{-1}]$ does not contain $\sqrt{t}$, so the $\mathbb{Z}/2$-cover $u^2=t$ is a genuinely non-trivial torsor, representing a nontrivial class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.

questions.q: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space?
choices: For $N=2$, $E[2]$ is not a group
For $N=2$, $[-1]$ acts as the identity on $E[2]$ (since $-P=P$ for $2$-torsion), so it still fixes every level-$2$ basis
For $N=2$, the curve $Y(2)$ does not exist
Because $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}/2)$ is trivial
questions.explain: On $E[2]$, multiplication by $-1$ is the identity because $2P=0$ implies $-P=P$. So $[-1]$ fixes any basis of $E[2]$, i.e. level-$2$ is not rigid enough. For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]\ne 1$ on $E[N]$.

questions.q: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$.
questions.explain: $3(4)-3=9$. This is the deformation-theoretic count $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$.

questions.q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$.

hard.q: Why does adding a level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$, turning the Deligne–Mumford stack into a representable functor (a scheme)?
choices: Level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ forces $\mathrm{Aut}(E,\alpha_N)=\{\mathrm{id}\}$ because $[-1]$ acts nontrivially on any basis of $E[N]$ when $N\ge 3$
Adding markings makes the moduli space proper
For $N\ge 3$ the modular curve $Y(N)$ has genus $\ge 2$
Level structure replaces $E$ by a finite flat group scheme
hard.hint: Level-$N$ with $N \ge 3$ kills the generic automorphism group.
hard.explain:
For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]
e\mathrm{id}$ on $E[N]\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^2$: it sends every basis vector $v$ to $-v
e v$ (since $N\ge 3$ means $-1
e 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}/N$). Hence $[-1]$ does not preserve any level-$N$ basis, so the automorphism group of a level-$N$-structured elliptic curve is trivial and the functor becomes representable.

hard.q: The boundary $\overline{M}_g\setminus M_g$ parametrises stable nodal curves. Which type of degeneration corresponds to an irreducible component of the boundary divisor $\Delta_0\subset\overline{M}_g$?
choices: A smooth curve of genus $g-1$
A smooth curve pinched at two points to form a single node, remaining irreducible (geometric genus $g-1$)
Two smooth curves of genera $h$ and $g-h$ joined at a node
A rational nodal curve with $g$ nodes
hard.hint: Irreducible nodal curves sit on the boundary of $\overline{M}_g$.
hard.explain: $\Delta_0$ consists of irreducible nodal curves: a smooth curve of genus $g-1$ with two points identified to create one node. Boundary divisors $\Delta_h$ for $h\ge 1$ consist of two components of genera $h$ and $g-h$ joined at a node.

questions.q: Compute $\dim M_g$ for $g=6$ using the Riemann–Roch formula $\dim M_g=3g-3$.
questions.explain: $3(6)-3=15$. This matches $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$, the first-order deformation space of a smooth genus-$6$ curve.

questions.q: A stable $n$-pointed curve $(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ of genus $g$ is required to have at-worst-nodal singularities, be proper and connected, and satisfy which finiteness condition?
choices: $\operatorname{Aut}(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ is trivial
$\operatorname{Aut}(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ is finite
every component is rational
$C$ is smooth
questions.explain: Stability is exactly finiteness of the automorphism group. Equivalent combinatorial conditions: every smooth rational component has $\ge 3$ special points, every genus-$1$ component has $\ge 1$. This is what cuts out a Deligne–Mumford stack.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a stable curve of genus $2$?
choices: a smooth genus-$2$ curve
two smooth elliptic curves joined at a node
a smooth rational curve $\mathbb{P}^1$ with no markings or nodes
a genus-$1$ curve with a node attached to a $\mathbb{P}^1$ carrying three marked points
questions.explain: A smooth $\mathbb{P}^1$ alone has arithmetic genus $0$, not $2$, and also has infinite automorphism group $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ — it fails stability on every count. The other options are standard boundary strata of $\overline{M}_2$ and $\overline{M}_{2,3}$.

hard.q: Order the steps in proving that $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is a proper Deligne–Mumford stack for $2g-2+n>0$:
choices: (1) Define stable $n$-pointed curves. (2) Show the moduli functor satisfies the valuative criterion of properness (every 1-parameter family over a punctured disk extends to a stable limit). (3) Conclude compactness via algebraicity.
(1) Triangulate the surface. (2) Apply Gauss–Bonnet. (3) Compactify by adding cusps.
(1) Take the GIT quotient. (2) Add boundary by taking closure in projective space. (3) Verify smoothness.
(1) Use the Riemann existence theorem. (2) Analytify. (3) Apply GAGA.
hard.hint: Geometric invariant theory first, then properness via stable reduction.
hard.explain: Deligne–Mumford's argument: first introduce the stable-curve notion (finiteness of $\mathrm{Aut}$), then verify the valuative criterion (semistable reduction theorem provides the extension), and deduce properness from the algebraic stack structure.

hard.q: A stable rational ($g=0$) curve with $n$ marked points exists only for $n\ge 3$ (stability condition $2\cdot0-2+n>0$ and finite $\mathrm{Aut}$). What is the minimum $n$ for which $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a non-empty proper scheme?
hard.hint: Automorphism groups become finite with three or more marked points.
hard.explain: For $g=0$, stability requires $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{P}^1,p_1,\dots,p_n)$ finite, which forces $n\ge 3$. $\overline{M}_{0,3}$ is a single point (three points on $\mathbb{P}^1$ are rigidified by $\mathrm{PGL}_2$), so $n=3$ is the minimum.

hard.q: Which of the following is a stable genus-$1$ curve with $1$ marked point (an element of $\overline{M}_{1,1}$)?
choices: A smooth elliptic curve $(E, p)$ with $p\in E$
A rational nodal cubic $C$ (an irreducible rational curve with one node) with the marked point $p$ at the smooth locus
A $\mathbb{P}^1$ with one marked point
Two $\mathbb{P}^1$s glued at two points with no markings
hard.hint: A stable genus-$1$ curve with one marked point is a nodal cubic with a smooth point.
hard.explain: A rational nodal cubic has arithmetic genus $1$ and one node. With a smooth marked point $p$, the automorphism group is finite (it equals $\mathbb{Z}/2$ in many cases), satisfying the stability condition. A $\mathbb{P}^1$ with one point has $\mathrm{Aut}=\mathbb{C}^*$ — infinite, hence not stable.
